revision.comment: Created
revision.text:
[[Image:Saikan1.jpg|thumb|right|250px|A twisting hallway in Saikan.]] '''Saikan''' (斎館, ''Saikan'') is a sprawling temple lodging atop '''[[Mt. Haguro]]''' (羽黒山 ''Haguro-san''), part of the '''[[Three Mountains of Dewa]]''' (出羽三山 ''Dewa Sanzan''). It connects directly to the main temple of the complex through a long passageway that climbs further up the mountain. Elaborate ''Gohonbo Bassho-zen'' meals are served to visitors. Traditionally, it is reached by climbing up 2,466 stone steps up the side of the mountain, but it can also be reached by car or bus.
<gallery> Image:Saikan2.jpg|A simple breakfast. Image:Saikan3.jpg|One of many large tatami rooms for guests. Image:Saikan4.jpg|Hallway. </gallery>
==External link== * [http://wikitravel.org/en/article/Dewa_Sanzan Wikitravel: Dewa Sanzan]
{{Yamagata-geo-stub}}
[[Category:Shinto shrines]]
[[de:Drei Berge von Dewa]] [[fr:Trois monts Dewa]] [[it:Tre montagne di Dewa]] [[ja:出羽三山]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Saikan (Hagurosan)]][[en:Saikan]][[simple:Saikan]] {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|'''Saikan'''|斎館}} | image = Saikan1.jpg | caption = A twisting hallway in Saikan. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|38.703364|139.979944|format=dms|display=title,inline|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} '''Saikan''' (斎館, ''Saikan'') is a sprawling [[:en:Shukubo|temple lodging]] atop [[:en:Mount Haguro|Mt. Haguro]] (羽黒山 ''Haguro-san''), part of the [[:en:Three Mountains of Dewa|Three Mountains of Dewa]] (出羽三山 ''Dewa Sanzan'') in [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=岩鼻通明 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XPczAAAAMAAJ |title=出羽三山信仰の歴史地理学的研究 |date=1992 |publisher=名著出版 |isbn=978-4-626-01430-6 |pages=3 |language=ja}}</ref> It was previously known as {{Nihongo3||華蔵院|Kezō-in}}.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DgZPAQAAMAAJ |title=日本の美術 |date=2005 |publisher=至文堂 |pages=84 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Earhart |first=H. Byron |date=1968 |title=The Celebration of "Haru-Yama" (Spring Mountain): An Example of Folk Religious Practices in Contemporary Japan |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1177798 |journal=Asian Folklore Studies |volume=27 |issue=1 |pages=1–24 |doi=10.2307/1177798 |jstor=1177798 |issn=0385-2342}}</ref>
It connects directly to the main temple of the complex through a long passageway that climbs further up the mountain. Elaborate ''Gohonbo Bassho-zen'' meals are served to visitors. Traditionally, it is reached by climbing up 2,466 stone steps up the side of the mountain, but it can also be reached by car or bus.
<gallery> File:Saikan2.jpg|A simple breakfast. File:Saikan3.jpg|One of many large [[:en:Washitsu|tatami rooms]] for guests. File:Saikan4.jpg|Hallway. </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
==External links== {{Wikivoyage|Dewa_Sanzan}}

Haguro]] (羽黒山 ''Haguro-san''), part of the [[:en:Three Mountains of Dewa|Three Mountains of Dewa]] (出羽三山 ''Dewa Sanzan'') in [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=岩鼻通明 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XPczAAAAMAAJ |title=出羽三山信仰の歴史地理学的研究 |date=1992 |publisher=名著出版 |isbn=978-4-626-01430-6 |pages=3 |language=ja}}</ref> It was previously known as {{Nihongo3||華蔵院|Kezō-in}}.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DgZPAQAAMAAJ |title=日本の美術 |date=2005 |publisher=至文堂 |pages=84 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Earhart |first=H. Byron |date=1968 |title=The Celebration of "Haru-Yama" (Spring Mountain): An Example of Folk Religious Practices in Contemporary Japan |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1177798 |journal=Asian Folklore Studies |volume=27 |issue=1 |pages=1–24 |doi=10.2307/1177798 |jstor=1177798 |issn=0385-2342}}</ref>
It connects directly to the main temple of the complex through a long passageway that climbs further up the mountain. Elaborate ''Gohonbo Bassho-zen'' meals are served to visitors. Traditionally, it is reached by climbing up 2,466 stone steps up the side of the mountain, but it can also be reached by car or bus.
<gallery> File:Saikan2.jpg|A simple breakfast. File:Saikan3.jpg|One of many large [[:en:Washitsu|tatami rooms]] for guests. File:Saikan4.jpg|Hallway. </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
==External links== {{Wikivoyage|Dewa_Sanzan}}

[[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamagata Prefecture]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Saikan (Hagurosan)]][[en:Saikan]][[simple:Saikan]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Saikan (Hagurosan)]][[en:Saikan]][[simple:Saikan]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Saikan (Hagurosan)]][[en:Saikan]][[simple:Saikan]] {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|'''Saikan'''|斎館}} | image = Saikan1.jpg | caption = A twisting hallway in Saikan. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|38.703364|139.979944|format=dms|display=title,inline|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} '''Saikan''' (斎館, ''Saikan'') is a sprawling [[:en:Shukubo|temple lodging]] atop [[:en:Mount Haguro|Mt. Haguro]] (羽黒山 ''Haguro-san''), part of the [[:en:Three Mountains of Dewa|Three Mountains of Dewa]] (出羽三山 ''Dewa Sanzan'') in [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=岩鼻通明 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XPczAAAAMAAJ |title=出羽三山信仰の歴史地理学的研究 |date=1992 |publisher=名著出版 |isbn=978-4-626-01430-6 |pages=3 |language=ja}}</ref> It was previously known as {{Nihongo3||華蔵院|Kezō-in}}.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DgZPAQAAMAAJ |title=日本の美術 |date=2005 |publisher=至文堂 |pages=84 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Earhart |first=H. Byron |date=1968 |title=The Celebration of "Haru-Yama" (Spring Mountain): An Example of Folk Religious Practices in Contemporary Japan |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1177798 |journal=Asian Folklore Studies |volume=27 |issue=1 |pages=1–24 |doi=10.2307/1177798 |jstor=1177798 |issn=0385-2342}}</ref>
It connects directly to the main temple of the complex through a long passageway that climbs further up the mountain. Elaborate ''Gohonbo Bassho-zen'' meals are served to visitors. Traditionally, it is reached by climbing up 2,466 stone steps up the side of the mountain, but it can also be reached by car or bus.
<gallery> File:Saikan2.jpg|A simple breakfast. File:Saikan3.jpg|One of many large [[:en:Washitsu|tatami rooms]] for guests. File:Saikan4.jpg|Hallway. </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
==External links== {{Wikivoyage|Dewa_Sanzan}}

Byron |date=1968 |title=The Celebration of "Haru-Yama" (Spring Mountain): An Example of Folk Religious Practices in Contemporary Japan |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1177798 |journal=Asian Folklore Studies |volume=27 |issue=1 |pages=1–24 |doi=10.2307/1177798 |jstor=1177798 |issn=0385-2342}}</ref>
It connects directly to the main temple of the complex through a long passageway that climbs further up the mountain. Elaborate ''Gohonbo Bassho-zen'' meals are served to visitors. Traditionally, it is reached by climbing up 2,466 stone steps up the side of the mountain, but it can also be reached by car or bus.

Byron |date=1968 |title=The Celebration of "Haru-Yama" (Spring Mountain): An Example of Folk Religious Practices in Contemporary Japan |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1177798 |journal=Asian Folklore Studies |volume=27 |issue=1 |pages=1–24 |doi=10.2307/1177798 |jstor=1177798 |issn=0385-2342}}</ref>
It connects directly to the main temple of the complex through a long passageway that climbs further up the mountain. Elaborate ''Gohonbo Bassho-zen'' meals are served to visitors. Traditionally, it is reached by climbing up 2,466 stone steps up the side of the mountain, but it can also be reached by car or bus.
<gallery> File:Saikan2.jpg|A simple breakfast. File:Saikan3.jpg|One of many large {{ill|Washitsu|en|Washitsu|WD=Q1347816|lt=tatami rooms}} for guests. File:Saikan4.jpg|Hallway. </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Saikan (Hagurosan)]][[en:Saikan]][[simple:Saikan]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Saikan (Hagurosan)]][[en:Saikan]][[simple:Saikan]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Saikan (Hagurosan)]][[en:Saikan]][[simple:Saikan]] {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|'''Saikan'''|斎館}} | image = Saikan1.jpg | caption = A twisting hallway in Saikan. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|38.703364|139.979944|format=dms|display=title,inline|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} '''Saikan''' (斎館, ''Saikan'') is a sprawling {{ill|Shukubo|en|Shukubo|WD=Q11455614|lt=temple lodging|1=Shukubo}} atop {{ill|Mount Haguro|en|Mount Haguro|WD=Q3090765|lt=Mt. Haguro|1=Mount Haguro}} (羽黒山 ''Haguro-san''), part of the {{ill|Three Mountains of Dewa|en|Three Mountains of Dewa|WD=Q1155726|lt=Three Mountains of Dewa|1=Three Mountains of Dewa}} (出羽三山 ''Dewa Sanzan'') in {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|WD=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture|1=Yamagata Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.<ref>{{Cite book |last=岩鼻通明 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XPczAAAAMAAJ |title=出羽三山信仰の歴史地理学的研究 |date=1992 |publisher=名著出版 |isbn=978-4-626-01430-6 |pages=3 |language=ja}}</ref> It was previously known as {{Nihongo3||華蔵院|Kezō-in}}.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DgZPAQAAMAAJ |title=日本の美術 |date=2005 |publisher=至文堂 |pages=84 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Earhart |first=H. Byron |date=1968 |title=The Celebration of "Haru-Yama" (Spring Mountain): An Example of Folk Religious Practices in Contemporary Japan |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1177798 |journal=Asian Folklore Studies |volume=27 |issue=1 |pages=1–24 |doi=10.2307/1177798 |jstor=1177798 |issn=0385-2342}}</ref>
It connects directly to the main temple of the complex through a long passageway that climbs further up the mountain. Elaborate ''Gohonbo Bassho-zen'' meals are served to visitors. Traditionally, it is reached by climbing up 2,466 stone steps up the side of the mountain, but it can also be reached by car or bus.
<gallery> File:Saikan2.jpg|A simple breakfast. File:Saikan3.jpg|One of many large {{ill|Washitsu|en|Washitsu|WD=Q1347816|lt=tatami rooms|1=Washitsu}} for guests. File:Saikan4.jpg|Hallway. </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
==External links== {{Wikivoyage|Dewa_Sanzan}}
